summary: Florida Education Commissioner Tony Bennett resigned after being caught manipulating Indiana's school grading system to benefit a school operated by a major GOP donor. Jordan Ellenberg exposed the fraud as a simple lie about missing zeroes in a weighted average calculation. O'Neil uses this scandal to argue that educational accountability systems are inherently vulnerable to gaming and manipulation, especially by those with political connections. She suggests that high-stakes testing incentivizes corruption, and that more complex models like Value-Added would make such manipulation even harder to detect. The solution, she argues, is to measure school performance without punitive consequences, using data to help struggling schools rather than punish them.
key_points: The Indiana school grading scandal shows how even simple mathematical models get manipulated for political benefit by those with connections
More complex accountability models like Value-Added are even easier to game because nobody can verify the calculations
High-stakes testing creates perverse incentives that make gaming and manipulation inevitable
Selection bias undermines claims about charter school success - experiments with motivated students, parents and teachers always look good
Removing punitive consequences and using measurement to help struggling schools would produce clearer, more honest signals
money_quotes: The lesson learned for corrupt politicians in the future may well to be sure the formulae are more complicated and thus easier to game.
Look no further than the Value-Added Model for teachers and schools, which literally nobody understands or could prove is being manipulated in any given instance.
The question of who gets the best opportunity to manipulate their scores is, as shown in this example of the GOP-donor-connected school, often a question of who has the best connections.
Instead of punishing schools with bad scores, we could see how they need help.

As any statistician could tell you, any time you have an 'educational experiment' involving highly motivated students, parents, and teachers, it will seem like a success. That's called selection bias.
The proof of the pudding lies in the scaling up of the method.
We need to think longer term and consider how we're treating good teachers and school administration who have to live under arbitrary and unfair systems. They might just leave.
key_insight: High-stakes educational accountability systems don't just fail to measure school quality accurately - they actively incentivize corruption and manipulation by those with political power, while the complexity of models like Value-Added makes such gaming impossible to detect.
